perturbation.original_text: Captaris agrees that, for a period ending eight weeks after the date of this letter agreement, it will afford Vector and its Representatives, reasonable access to the information described in Exhibit A and the books, contracts, commitments and records of Captaris and its subsidiaries relevant thereto, unless and until Vector and Captaris have reached a non-binding consensus regarding basic deal terms, at which time access will broaden to include all other information concerning the business, properties and personnel (subject to restrictions imposed by 3 applicable law) of Captaris and its Subsidiaries as Vector may reasonably request and that is in the possession or control of Captaris.
perturbation.changed_text: Captaris agrees that, for a period ending two weeks after the date of this letter agreement, it will afford Vector and its Representatives, reasonable access to the information described in Exhibit A and the books, contracts, commitments and records of Captaris and its subsidiaries relevant thereto, unless and until Vector and Captaris have reached a non-binding consensus regarding basic deal terms, at which time access will broaden to include all other information concerning the business, properties and personnel (subject to restrictions imposed by 3 applicable law) of Captaris and its Subsidiaries as Vector may reasonably request and that is in the possession or control of Captaris.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the access period to two weeks, which might be insufficient for proper due diligence depending on the complexity of the transaction. This could be seen as a violation of good faith negotiation principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law, where the contract is governed, implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Reducing the due diligence period to two weeks could be argued as a breach of this covenant if it prevents Vector from properly assessing the target company, thereby undermining the purpose of the agreement.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the due diligence period from eight weeks to two weeks. The Law Explanation suggests this may violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The Perturbation Explanation states this could undermine the agreement's purpose if it prevents proper assessment. Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2 both state that 'Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' Reducing the access period significantly impedes the ability to perform due diligence in good faith, potentially undermining the whole deal and violating the good faith obligation, establishing a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Vector hereby agrees that, for a period of twelve months from the date hereof, it will not, and will not encourage or assist its affiliates to, solicit for employment (a) any officers or other senior key employees of Captaris or any of its business units so long as they are employed by the Captaris or its business units, without obtaining the prior written consent of Captaris, and (b) any of the other employees of Captaris or any of its business units with whom Vector has had contact or who Vector first became aware of during the period of its investigation of Captaris, so long as they are employed by the Captaris or its business units, without obtaining the prior written consent of Captaris.
perturbation.changed_text: Vector hereby agrees that, for a period of five years from the date hereof, it will not, and will not encourage or assist its affiliates to, solicit for employment (a) any officers or other senior key employees of Captaris or any of its business units so long as they are employed by the Captaris or its business units, without obtaining the prior written consent of Captaris, and (b) any of the other employees of Captaris or any of its business units with whom Vector has had contact or who Vector first became aware of during the period of its investigation of Captaris, so long as they are employed by the Captaris or its business units, without obtaining the prior written consent of Captaris.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to five years is likely an unreasonably long duration and may be considered an unenforceable restraint of trade under California law, where Captaris is located.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_citation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. A five-year non-solicitation agreement is likely an unreasonable restraint on trade, especially given the broad scope of employees covered. While there are narrow exceptions, this broad restriction is likely unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period from twelve months to five years. The provided law snippet from California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The Law Explanation states that a five-year non-solicitation agreement is likely an unreasonable restraint on trade, especially given the broad scope of employees covered. The Perturbation Explanation also suggests that the extended period is likely an unreasonably long duration and may be considered an unenforceable restraint of trade under California law. Therefore, the modification directly contradicts the legal principle against unreasonable restraints of trade as codified in BPC 16600, making the contract potentially void.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of litigation relating to this letter agreement, if a court of competent jurisdiction determines in a final, non-appealable order that a party has breached this letter agreement, then such party shall be liable and pay to the non-breaching party the reasonable legal fees incurred by such non-breaching party in connection with such litigation, including any appeal therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of litigation relating to this letter agreement, the prevailing party shall not be entitled to attorney's fees, regardless of any breach determined by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Denying attorney's fees to the prevailing party, regardless of a breach, may conflict with statutory provisions that allow for attorney's fees in certain contract disputes or where a party has acted in bad faith, depending on the specifics of the case and the jurisdiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021
perturbation.law_citation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021
perturbation.law_explanation: While California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021 generally allows parties to agree on attorney's fees, certain statutes, such as those related to wage claims or consumer protection, may override such agreements and allow for attorney's fees to the prevailing party, regardless of the contract's provision. This complete denial could be problematic.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1021 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly denies attorney's fees to the prevailing party, regardless of a breach. This contradicts the general principle outlined in California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021 (Snippet 1), which states that attorney's fees are generally subject to the agreement of the parties, 'except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute'. The Law Explanation suggests that certain statutes (e.g., wage claims, consumer protection) may override contractual agreements regarding attorney's fees, regardless of the contract's provisions. Therefore, denying attorney's fees outright, even to a prevailing party, creates a legal contradiction, particularly if a statute mandates such fees in specific scenarios.

perturbation.original_text: This letter agreement is for the benefit of the parties and their respective directors, officers, stockholders, owners, affiliates, and agents, and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This letter agreement is for the benefit of the parties only and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, excluding its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California, while seemingly innocuous, opens the door to challenges regarding the enforceability of certain clauses if they are more restrictive than allowed under California law, particularly regarding non-compete or non-solicitation provisions, given that Captaris is located in California.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_citation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: By switching the governing law to California, Business and Professions Code Section 16600 becomes more directly applicable. If any clause within the agreement acts as a restraint of trade that would be invalid under California law (such as an overly broad non-solicitation clause), this change exacerbates the potential legal contradiction. Even though New York law might permit certain restrictive covenants, California's strong public policy against them will now govern.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from New York to California introduces a legal contradiction because California Business and Professions Code Section 16600(a) states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." This contradicts the original text's potential allowance of broader restrictions permissible under New York law, particularly regarding non-compete or non-solicitation provisions. Therefore, the modified text creates a situation where clauses valid under the original governing law could be deemed unenforceable under California law.

perturbation.original_text: This letter agreement will terminate on the second anniversary of the date of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This letter agreement will terminate upon the earlier of (i) the closing of a transaction between the parties, or (ii) six months from the date of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the agreement to six months creates potential issues if sensitive information has been shared and no deal occurs. This could violate general fiduciary duty or create an unfair advantage if one party has used the information improperly, especially considering the initial agreement was for a longer period.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Under New York law, all contracts carry an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Terminating the agreement prematurely, after confidential information has been exchanged, could be seen as acting in bad faith if it allows Vector to exploit the information gained during the due diligence period for its own purposes, especially when the original intent was to maintain confidentiality for a longer duration. If Vector uses this information within the 6 month timeframe to the detriment of Captaris, that's an issue.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a shorter termination period (six months) compared to the original two years. This raises concerns about potential exploitation of confidential information exchanged during the agreement. The Law Explanation argues that premature termination could be seen as acting in bad faith if it allows a party to exploit information gained. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, both citing UCC 1-304, state that every contract imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. By shortening the termination period, the modified contract could be interpreted as creating an opportunity for one party to unfairly benefit from the shared information, thereby contradicting the implied covenant of good faith. The original contract was for a longer period and the explanation states that the shorter period might create an unfair advantage. This represents an inconsistency that creates a legal contradiction.
